title: Raisin scones
subtitle: Classic fruit scones with plumped raisins
excerpt: Raisin scones are the British standard fruit scone: self-raising flour, butter, sugar, buttermilk, and raisins soaked to plump them. They should rise high and straight with a level top, split cleanly at the waist, and have an even, fine crumb. The no-twist cutter rule is not a superstition; it does affect the rise.
sourceNotes: Fruit scones are a staple of British baking and afternoon tea. The recipe is documented in British domestic cookery manuals throughout the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Isabella Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg, includes scone recipes. The form here follows the standard British fruit scone formula.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked scones and reheat from frozen in a 160°C oven for 12 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Scones are best on the day. Warm briefly in the oven if baked ahead.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; the high heat is what gives scones their height

content.text: Raisin scones are flour, cold butter, sugar, and buttermilk brought together briefly, with raisins soaked first to soften them. The dough is handled only as much as it takes to combine; overworking produces a tight, heavy scone. Cut straight down with a 5 cm cutter (no twisting) and bake high and fast. A perfectly made scone rises level, splits cleanly at the middle, and has a close, even crumb.

items.prepNote: soaked in hot water for 10 minutes, drained

content.text: Sift the flour and baking powder into a large bowl. Add the cold butter and rub in until the mixture looks like coarse breadcrumbs with some pea-sized pieces of butter remaining. Stir in the sugar and drained raisins. Add most of the buttermilk and bring together with a butter knife or palette knife in 8 to 10 strokes. Add the remaining buttermilk if there is any dry flour. The dough should be soft but not wet, and barely holding together.

Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Turn the dough out onto a lightly floured surface and pat gently to a thickness of about 3 cm; do not roll. Cut rounds with a 5 cm cutter dipped in flour. Press straight down and lift straight up; do not twist. Place close together on a lined baking tray. Brush the tops with beaten egg. Bake for 13 to 15 minutes until risen, golden on top, and the sides look matte and set. Cool on a wire rack for 10 minutes before splitting.
